questions.scenario:
The orders API on EC2 calls S3 and fails:

AccessDenied is arn:aws:sts::123456789012:assumed-role/devpolaris-orders-api-ec2/i-0f2c4a91. Is not authorized to perform s3 is PutObject on arn:aws:s3:::devpolaris-orders-exports/prod/orders.csv.

What should change?

options.text: Attach the S3-capable instance profile but leave the private subnet without an S3 endpoint or other outbound route.
Put long-lived access keys in user data so bootstrapping can reach S3 before the application starts.
Review the instance role policy and add the narrow missing permission if the app should write that object.
Move the instance to a public subnet and attach an instance profile, using the internet gateway for S3 access.

questions.explanation:
The error names the assumed instance role, so IAM permission is the first layer. EC2 software should use the instance profile instead of copied static keys. Network placement and SSH keys are separate concerns.

Learn more in [IAM roles for Amazon EC2](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/iam-roles-for-amazon-ec2.html).

questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris orders process on EC2 starts under systemd, but the service must survive reboot and expose the application on the expected port. The unit file contains this configuration.

```text
Environment=PORT=8080
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node server.js
```

Target group settings: Protocol is HTTP. Port is 3000. Health path is /health.

The app log says `listening on 8080`. What should the team do?

options.text: Increase desired EC2 instances because port mismatch is solved by more capacity.
Change NODE_ENV to development so Express listens on both ports.
Rotate the instance role because IAM chooses the health check port.
Align the runtime port contract by changing the app environment or the target group so both use the same port.

questions.explanation:
The evidence shows a simple contract mismatch. The ALB checks port 3000 while the process listens on 8080. Fix the runtime or target group mapping deliberately, then recheck local and target health.

Learn more in [Amazon EC2 concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/concepts.html).

questions.scenario: The DevPolaris orders API is healthy when tested locally on its EC2 instance, but one request fails when the application reads a Systems Manager parameter. The AWS error identifies the caller as the instance profile session for `i-0f2c4a91`, the denied action as `ssm:GetParameter`, and the resource as `/prod/orders/public-base-url`. What should the engineer inspect first?

options.text: The EC2 instance role policy and the exact denied SSM parameter ARN.
The parameter value format, because malformed text is reported as an IAM authorization denial.
The systemd service account, because Linux user permissions determine `ssm:GetParameter` authorization.
The instance security group egress rule, because blocked HTTPS is reported with an IAM principal and action.

questions.explanation:
The error provides the IAM decision tuple: caller, action, and resource. The EC2 instance role is the application's AWS identity, so its identity policy and the exact parameter ARN are the first checks. Linux service users, security groups, and parameter formatting do not produce an IAM authorization denial naming `ssm:GetParameter`.

Learn more in [IAM roles for Amazon EC2](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/iam-roles-for-amazon-ec2.html).

questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris orders process is healthy when tested locally on EC2, but the load balancer cannot reach it. The engineer compares process binding, target configuration, and network rules.

Systemctl status is Loaded enabled, Active active (running), Main PID 1842 (node). Journal is service=devpolaris-orders-api port=3000 node_env=production. Local curl /health is 200. ALB target health is healthy.

Which facts are useful proof that port 3000 is being served correctly?

options.text: A local health request to 127.0.0.1:3000 returns success.
The SSH key pair being configured proves the health endpoint is correct.
systemd sees the service active with a Node.js main process.
The ALB marks the instance target healthy on the expected port.
The instance has a public IPv4 address, so the target group can bypass the instance security group for health checks.

questions.explanation:
You want evidence from both inside and outside the instance. systemd proves the process is alive, local curl proves the app answers on the port, and target health proves the ALB can reach it. The SSH key name does not prove runtime health.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer target health](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/target-group-health-checks.html).

questions.scenario:
After a sale starts, the kernel logs show:

Out of memory is Killed process 1842 (node) total-vm:812000kB anon-rss:621000kB. Systemd is devpolaris-orders-api.service: Main process exited, status=9/KILL. ALB target health is Target.Timeout.

Which operating decision is most relevant?

options.text: Increase the instance size from the single out-of-memory event before checking which process, limit, or workload caused it.
Right-size the instance or reduce memory pressure, because the server is killing the Node.js process under load.
Increase the instance size because an out-of-memory kill is sufficient evidence that sustained demand exceeds capacity.
Reduce the process memory limit and restart immediately so the kernel chooses a different victim on the next spike.

questions.explanation:
The kernel killed the process because memory ran out. systemd can restart it, but repeated OOM events mean the capacity or memory behavior needs attention. The ALB timeout is a symptom after the process disappears.

Learn more in [EC2 status checks](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/monitoring-system-instance-status-check.html).

questions.scenario:
A DevPolaris EC2 instance serves the application locally, but the internet-facing ALB reports timeouts. The engineer compares the listener, target, and security-group path.

Systemctl is-active devpolaris-orders-api is active. Curl http is //127.0.0.1:3000/health: {"status":"ok"}. ALB target health is unhealthy Target.Timeout. Instance SG inbound is TCP 3000 from sg-admin-bastion.

What is the next best fix direction?

options.text: Allow the ALB security group on port 3000 but keep the Node.js process bound to `127.0.0.1`.
Change the target group port to 443 and terminate TLS again on the instance to match the public listener.
Expose port 3000 to the VPC CIDR so health checks and operator tests can use the same broad path.
Adjust the instance security group to allow TCP 3000 from the ALB security group.

questions.explanation:
The service is healthy locally, so the broken part is outside the process. The security group only allows the bastion, not the ALB, to reach port 3000. Fix that path and then recheck target health.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
DevPolaris customers receive 504 responses while the EC2 process remains active. The on-call engineer examines application and host evidence before restarting or resizing.

ALB target health is unhealthy Target.Timeout. Systemctl is-active devpolaris-orders-api is active. Curl http is //127.0.0.1:3000/health: 200. Security group is TCP 3000 from sg-admin-bastion only.

What should the operator do first?

options.text: Fix the ALB-to-instance network path, especially the security group source for TCP 3000.
Restart the active systemd service because local health success means systemd is stale.
Change the health route to /checkout because 504s are application validation errors.
Check receipt-email Lambda concurrency first, because another checkout component sometimes creates 504 symptoms.

questions.explanation:
The local service is answering, so the problem is likely between the ALB and instance. The security group evidence is the strongest clue. Restarting a healthy local service would not open the path from the load balancer.

Learn more in [EC2 user data](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/user-data.html).
